Paper Title/Study: Mixed vitamin C and zinc diet supplements co-administered with artemether drug improved haematological profile and survival of mice infected with Plasmodium berghei
Authors: Felicia N. Ekeh, Nkiru E. Ekechukwu, Chimdinma F. Chukwuma, Ifeanyi Oscar N. Aguzie, Chinenye M. Ohanu, Chike Ebido, Stanley N. Oluah
Diet Type: Formulated diet with varying ratios of Vitamin C and Zinc
Biomarkers: PCV, HB, RBC, WBC, MCV, MCH, MCHC, parasitaemia
Outcome / Finding: Improved haematological profile (PCV, HB) and reduced mortality.
Contribution: Problem: To evaluate if vitamin C and zinc supplements co-administered with artemether improve haematological outcomes and survival in malaria-infected mice.
Contribution: Result: Supplementation improved PCV and HB, and lowered mortality rate, though it did not reduce parasitaemia. The 30:70 vitamin C-zinc ratio performed best.

Paper Title/Study: Not explicitly provided (cited as Rahfiludin and Ginandjar [19])
Biomarkers: haematological outcomes, haemoglobin concentration, haematocrit concentrations
Contribution: Problem: Effect of combined vitamin C and zinc supplement on malaria patients.
Contribution: Result: 45 days of supplementation resulted in positive haematological outcomes; haemoglobin and haematocrit concentrations were significantly improved.

Paper Title/Study: Not explicitly provided (cited as Sazawal et al. [17])
Contribution: Problem: Risk of direct iron supplement during malaria.
Contribution: Result: Reported increased risk for severe illness from direct iron supplement during malaria.

Paper Title/Study: Not explicitly provided (cited as Darling et al. [37])
Contribution: Problem: Impact of zinc supplement on placental malaria.
Contribution: Result: Reduction in histopathology-positive placental malaria among pregnant women who received zinc supplement.
